Okay, so I need to solve this problem about four students who each have a different number from a set S of four consecutive two-digit integers. The teacher told them that some number is divisible by 6 and another is divisible by 7. When the teacher first asked if anyone could figure out the set S, all four said no. But then, after hearing that everyone said no, they all could figure it out. I need to find the sum of all possible values of the greatest element of S.

First, let me parse the problem again. The set S has four consecutive two-digit numbers. So, for example, S could be {10,11,12,13}, but not just any four numbers, they have to be consecutive. Also, one of them is divisible by 6, and a different one is divisible by 7. The students each have one number from S, different from each other. When asked if they can figure out S, all say no. But then they can figure it out after hearing that all said no. The question is to find the sum of all possible greatest elements in such sets S.

So, first step: figure out all possible sets S of four consecutive two-digit integers where exactly one is divisible by 6 and exactly one is divisible by 7. So maybe there is at least one divisible by 6 and at least one divisible by 7, possibly more? But since they are consecutive numbers, maybe not? Let me think.

If there are four consecutive numbers, how many multiples of 6 can there be? Well, multiples of 6 are 6 apart. So in four consecutive numbers, there can be at most one multiple of 6. Similarly for multiples of 7, since 7 is larger than 4, there can be at most one multiple of 7 in four consecutive numbers. So actually, the problem is saying that there is exactly one multiple of 6 and exactly one multiple of 7 in the set S. So that simplifies things.

Therefore, S is four consecutive two-digit integers with exactly one multiple of 6 and exactly one multiple of 7.

So first, I need to generate all such sets S. Then, for each of these sets, figure out whether when each student is given one of the numbers, they can deduce S based on their own number and the information given. Since all four students said no initially, that tells us that for each number in S, there exists another set S' that contains that number, also satisfying the conditions (four consecutive two-digit numbers with a multiple of 6 and a multiple of 7), such that the student cannot determine S from their number alone. Then, after all four say no, they can deduce S. Therefore, the sets S must be such that once you know that no one could deduce S from their own number, then you can figure it out. So this is similar to some logic puzzles where information is revealed through others' inability to know.

Therefore, the problem is similar to the "sum and product" puzzle. The key is to find the sets S where the initial knowledge is insufficient, but after knowing that others also don't know, they can figure it out.
